reasoning: I will provide a concise summary of the article, capturing the key facts about funding cuts, their stated impacts on student numbers and staff, and the comments from officials.
output: Queen's University in Northern Ireland faces an £8m subsidy cut from the Department of Employment and Learning, with the reduction taking effect from September 2015. The university plans to cut undergraduate places by about 1,010 over three years and anticipates job losses among both academic and non-academic staff, though there will be no compulsory redundancies. The institution has around 17,000 full-time undergraduate and postgraduate students and about 3,800 staff, with an intake of roughly 4,500 undergraduates per year. While there are no immediate plans to close departments or courses, some may be at risk due to funding pressures. Education Minister Stephen Farry warned that some students might study elsewhere in the UK as a result, and Queen's vice-chancellor Patrick Johnston cautioned that the cuts could damage the university’s reputation and local economy. The broader context includes a reduction in DEL funding and a separate move by Ulster University to drop 53 courses, with further job and place cuts expected there as well.
